visits.chief_complaint: Annual physical and blood pressure medication refill.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Good morning, Sarah. Good to see you again. How have things been since your last visit?

Patient: Pretty good overall, I think. Just here for the annual and to get my lisinopril refilled.

Doctor: Sure, we can take care of that. Your blood pressure today is 134 over 82, which is in the same range as last year. Are you taking the lisinopril every morning?

Patient: Yes, 20 milligrams, every day with breakfast.

Doctor: Great. Any side effects? Cough, dizziness, anything like that?

Patient: No, no cough. I do feel a little tired sometimes, but I figured that's just being 58 and working too much.

Doctor: Tell me a bit more about the tiredness. When did you start noticing it?

Patient: Maybe the past few months? It's hard to say. I'll get home from work and just feel kind of drained. Some days are worse than others.

Doctor: How's your sleep?

Patient: Fine, I think. I get about seven hours. My husband says I don't snore badly or anything.

Doctor: Any new stress at work or at home?

Patient: Work is busy, you know how it is. My daughter started college in August, so the house is quieter. That's been an adjustment.

Doctor: Sure. How about exercise — are you still walking?

Patient: I try to get out three or four times a week. Maybe thirty minutes around the neighborhood. I've been a little less consistent the last month or two.

Doctor: That's still good. Any chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath?

Patient: Um, no, not really. I get winded if I rush up the stairs but I think that's just being out of shape.

Doctor: Any swelling in your legs or ankles?

Patient: No, nothing like that.

Doctor: How about your diet? Still trying to keep the salt down?

Patient: I'm pretty careful at home. We eat out maybe once a week.

Doctor: Good. I'm going to order the usual labs today — fasting lipid panel, CBC, metabolic panel, A1c. Given the fatigue, let's check thyroid too, and a B12. Sometimes those slip when we're not looking.

Patient: That sounds reasonable.

Doctor: And your father — he had his heart attack at what age?

Patient: He was 64. He's doing okay now, but yes, there's heart disease on his side.

Doctor: Okay. We'll keep that in mind. Mammogram up to date?

Patient: Yes, I had it in May. Normal.

Doctor: Colonoscopy?

Patient: Done last year, also normal.

Doctor: Good. Let's plan to follow up in a couple of months once the labs are back, and we can talk about whether we need to adjust anything. If the tiredness gets worse, or you notice anything new — chest discomfort, shortness of breath, anything that doesn't feel right — call sooner.

Patient: Will do.

Doctor: Take care, Sarah.

Patient: Thanks, doctor.

visits.chief_complaint: Lab follow-up and new occasional chest discomfort.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Hi Sarah, come on in. Your BP today is 136 over 84. How have you been feeling?

Patient: Mostly okay. The fatigue is still kind of there, but I wanted to mention something else. A couple of times in the last month I've had this — I don't know, like a tightness in my chest. It doesn't last long.

Doctor: Tell me more about that. Where exactly?

Patient: Right here, kind of central, maybe a little to the left. It's not really a pain, more like a heaviness.

Doctor: How long does it last?

Patient: A few minutes. Maybe five, ten at most. Then it just goes away on its own.

Doctor: Does anything seem to bring it on?

Patient: That's the thing, I'm not sure. One time I was just sitting at my desk. Another time I was loading the dishwasher. It doesn't feel like it's connected to anything specific.

Doctor: Does it happen with exercise?

Patient: I haven't really noticed it on my walks, no.

Doctor: Any associated symptoms — sweating, nausea, shortness of breath, lightheadedness?

Patient: No, nothing like that.

Doctor: Does it radiate anywhere — to your arm, your jaw?

Patient: No.

Doctor: How about any relationship to meals? Worse after eating, or when you lie down?

Patient: Hmm. Maybe a little after dinner sometimes? I do get heartburn now and then.

Doctor: Okay. So the labs from last visit looked mostly good. A1c is 5.6, so prediabetic range but not diabetic. Thyroid is normal, B12 is fine. Your LDL came back at 156, which is up from where it was. Total cholesterol is 232.

Patient: That's higher than before.

Doctor: It is. With your family history and your blood pressure, I'd like to start you on a statin. Atorvastatin, 20 milligrams at bedtime. It's well tolerated, generic, very effective.

Patient: Okay, I trust your judgment.

Doctor: As for the chest discomfort — given how it sounds, with the heartburn, the variability, and the brief duration, this could be reflux, or musculoskeletal, or even some anxiety. But because of your risk factors, I want to be careful. Let's get an EKG today, in the office. If anything looks off, we'll go from there.

Patient: That sounds fine.

Doctor: Are you still walking?

Patient: Yes, three times a week or so.

Doctor: Any smoking, alcohol?

Patient: No smoking, never. Glass of wine maybe twice a week.

Doctor: Good. Let's also try a trial of omeprazole for a few weeks, see if that helps with the chest discomfort. If it does, that's reassuring.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: I want you to call right away if the discomfort changes — gets worse, lasts longer, comes with shortness of breath or sweating, anything dramatic. Don't try to ride it out.

Patient: Got it.

Doctor: Let's plan to see you again in two months. We'll recheck your lipids in three.

Patient: Sounds good.

Doctor: Take care.

visits.chief_complaint: Follow-up of chest discomfort and persistent fatigue.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Hi Sarah. How are you doing today?

Patient: Doing okay. The omeprazole has helped some, I think. The heartburn is better.

Doctor: BP today is 132 over 80. That's good. And the chest discomfort — has it changed?

Patient: It's still happening, maybe two or three times since I saw you last. Not a lot, but it's there.

Doctor: Same character as before?

Patient: Yeah, the heaviness. Lasts a few minutes. Last time it happened I was carrying groceries up to the porch. That one was a little more noticeable.

Doctor: Did it stop when you put the groceries down?

Patient: Now that you mention it — yeah, I sat down for a minute and it eased up.

Doctor: Okay. The EKG from last visit was normal, and the omeprazole helping somewhat would suggest GERD is playing a role. But I want to be thorough. How is your tolerance for activity? Walking up stairs, hills?

Patient: Stairs are fine, but I have noticed I'm a little more out of breath than I used to be when I'm walking up the hill on Maple Street. There's a steep stretch.

Doctor: How long has that been going on?

Patient: Months, I guess. I figured I was just deconditioning.

Doctor: Are you still on the lisinopril and the atorvastatin?

Patient: Yes, both daily. No side effects.

Doctor: Any muscle aches?

Patient: No, none.

Doctor: Good. Let me listen to your heart and lungs. (pause) Heart sounds are regular, no murmurs. Lungs are clear. No swelling in your legs.

Patient: That's a relief.

Doctor: Let me ask you — and I know this sounds dramatic, but it isn't — when this happens, the chest heaviness, do you ever notice your jaw, your shoulder, anything in your back?

Patient: No, just the chest itself.

Doctor: Sweating, nausea, lightheadedness during?

Patient: No.

Doctor: Have you tried timing how long it lasts?

Patient: Not really. It's never been long enough to feel like an emergency.

Doctor: Here's where I am. Given your age, the family history, the blood pressure, the cholesterol, and now this discomfort that may be linked to exertion — I think it's worth getting an exercise stress test. It's a non-invasive way for us to see how your heart handles work.

Patient: Do you think something's wrong?

Doctor: I think it's most likely a combination of GERD, mild deconditioning, maybe some anxiety. But the picture isn't crystal clear, and with your risk profile, I'd rather know than guess.

Patient: Okay. That makes sense.

Doctor: I'll put the order in. The cardiology lab will call to schedule. In the meantime, keep taking everything as prescribed. If the discomfort happens, take note of what you were doing, how long it lasted, what made it stop. That information helps us a lot.

Patient: I'll keep a little log.

Doctor: Perfect. Anything else going on?

Patient: My daughter's home for break. Otherwise, no, just normal life.

Doctor: Good. I'll see you in a couple of months, sooner if anything changes.

Patient: Thanks.

visits.chief_complaint: Follow-up after negative exercise stress test; ongoing chest tightness.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Hi Sarah. Good to see you. How have you been?

Patient: I've been okay. The stress test results came in — they said it was normal?

Doctor: Yes, the cardiologist who read it said the test was negative. You hit your target heart rate and there were no significant changes on the EKG during exercise. That's reassuring.

Patient: Good. Although, I have to say, I haven't been feeling great. I'm still getting that chest tightness, and I think it's a little more often.

Doctor: Tell me what's been happening.

Patient: It's maybe once a week now, sometimes twice. Almost always when I'm doing something — taking out the trash, walking the dog up that hill. It comes on, I stop, it goes away. Maybe two or three minutes.

Doctor: Anything different about it?

Patient: It feels a tiny bit stronger than before. Like, I notice it more. Not painful exactly, but I can't ignore it.

Doctor: BP today is 138 over 86. A little higher than last time. Pulse is 78. How's your medication compliance?

Patient: Every day. I'm pretty good about that.

Doctor: How about salt, weight, the usual stuff?

Patient: Weight is about the same. I've been a little less active because honestly the chest thing makes me worried to push myself.

Doctor: That's an understandable response. Let me listen. (pause) Heart still regular, lungs clear. Any leg swelling?

Patient: No.

Doctor: Palpitations, racing heart?

Patient: No.

Doctor: I'm thinking about this carefully. Your stress test was negative, which is good, but treadmill stress tests aren't perfect — they can miss things, especially in women. Your symptoms have a pattern that fits with effort. I'd like to talk about a couple of things.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: First, I want to repeat your lipids today and see where the LDL has come down to. Second, I'd like to bump your lisinopril up to 30 milligrams. Your blood pressure has been creeping. Third — I want to keep a close eye on these episodes. If they keep increasing in frequency or intensity, we'll get cardiology more directly involved.

Patient: Should I be worried?

Doctor: I want you to be aware, not worried. Most likely this is a combination of factors, but I'm not willing to dismiss it. Have you had any episodes at rest recently?

Patient: No, only when I'm doing something.

Doctor: That's actually useful information. If you start having them at rest, or if they last longer than fifteen minutes, or come with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea — you go to the ER. Don't drive yourself.

Patient: Understood.

Doctor: Are you still drinking, smoking — anything new?

Patient: Same as before. Glass of wine sometimes. No smoking.

Doctor: How's the sleep, mood?

Patient: I've been a little anxious about all this, honestly. But sleep is okay.

Doctor: That's natural. We'll figure this out. Let's recheck in about eight weeks. Sooner if anything changes.

Patient: Thank you, doctor.

Doctor: Take care, Sarah.

visits.chief_complaint: Worsening exertional chest discomfort and shortness of breath.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Sarah, hi. Come on in. Tell me what's going on.

Patient: I think things are getting worse. The chest tightness is more often now. And I'm getting short of breath more easily. I really notice it when I'm walking. Even just to the mailbox and back, sometimes.

Doctor: Okay. Let's slow down and go through this. BP is 142 over 88, pulse 82. How frequent are the chest episodes now?

Patient: Several times a week. Maybe four or five.

Doctor: And the shortness of breath — is it always with the chest discomfort, or separate?

Patient: It comes together usually. Like, I'll be walking, I'll feel the heaviness, and I notice I'm breathing harder than I should be.

Doctor: How long does it take to recover when you stop?

Patient: A couple minutes. It's predictable, I guess. I stop, I rest, it goes away.

Doctor: Have you had any episodes at rest, or while sleeping?

Patient: One time last week I woke up and felt — I don't know, kind of pressure in my chest? But I'd been having a stressful dream. It went away pretty quickly.

Doctor: How quickly?

Patient: Maybe five minutes.

Doctor: Did you sit up, walk around?

Patient: I just laid there. I think I went back to sleep.

Doctor: Okay. Any swelling, palpitations?

Patient: No swelling. My heart hasn't felt like it's racing.

Doctor: Let me listen. (pause) Heart sounds regular, no murmurs or extra sounds. Lungs are clear. No edema.

Patient: That's good?

Doctor: Yes. But Sarah, the pattern you're describing is concerning enough that I want to refer you to cardiology directly. The symptoms are reproducible with exertion, and the frequency is going up. I don't want to wait.

Patient: Is this — is this a heart attack thing?

Doctor: It's not a heart attack right now. What you're describing sounds like it could be stable angina — narrowing in one of the heart's blood vessels that limits flow when you exert yourself. It's something we can manage, often very effectively. But we need to confirm what's going on.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: I'm going to put the cardiology referral in today. They'll likely want to do additional imaging — possibly a stress echo or a CT angiogram, depending on their judgment. Your previous treadmill test was negative, but as I mentioned, that doesn't rule everything out, especially in women.

Patient: How long until I can get in?

Doctor: I'll mark it as urgent so we can get you in within a couple of weeks. In the meantime, I want to start you on aspirin — 81 milligrams daily. And I'm going to give you a prescription for sublingual nitroglycerin. If the chest discomfort comes on, you place one under your tongue. If it doesn't go away in five minutes, take a second one. If it doesn't go away in fifteen minutes total, you call 911.

Patient: Okay. That's a lot.

Doctor: I know. Take it slowly, but take it seriously. No driving yourself if symptoms come on. Avoid heavy lifting and intense exertion until we know more. Light walking is fine.

Patient: I will.

Doctor: How are you doing emotionally with this?

Patient: A little scared. But I'd rather know.

Doctor: That's the right way to look at it. We're going to work through this together. Cardiology will have my notes. Call me anytime.

Patient: Thank you.

visits.chief_complaint: Interim follow-up; awaiting CT coronary angiogram.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Hi Sarah, how are you?

Patient: Hanging in there. I saw cardiology three weeks ago. They did the stress echo — said something looked a little abnormal but they want to do more imaging. I'm scheduled for a CT angiogram next week.

Doctor: Yes, I got Dr. Patel's note. The stress echo showed some wall motion changes in the inferior wall at peak exercise — suggestive of ischemia but not definitive. The CT angiogram will give us a much clearer picture of the coronary arteries themselves.

Patient: Yeah, that's what they said.

Doctor: How have your symptoms been since I saw you?

Patient: A little better, actually. They started me on a beta blocker — metoprolol, 25 milligrams twice a day. And I've been using the nitroglycerin maybe twice. The chest tightness is less frequent.

Doctor: That's reassuring. Any side effects from the metoprolol? Fatigue, dizziness, cold hands?

Patient: Maybe a little extra tired in the mornings? But it's manageable.

Doctor: Your BP today is 124 over 76, which is much better. Pulse is 64. Both look great. Are you taking everything — lisinopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, metoprolol?

Patient: All of them. Plus the omeprazole still, sometimes.

Doctor: Good. Any chest discomfort at rest in the last couple of weeks?

Patient: No, only with effort, and less than before.

Doctor: Shortness of breath?

Patient: Better. I can walk around without thinking about it as much.

Doctor: That's good. The medications are doing some of their job. The CT angiogram next week will tell us whether there's significant blockage that might need further intervention — possibly a heart catheterization or a stent.

Patient: That word still scares me.

Doctor: I know. But these procedures have come a long way. They're often outpatient, and the outcomes are very good when we catch things at this stage — before any heart damage has occurred.

Patient: My father had bypass surgery when he was 70. He came through it fine, lived another twelve years.

Doctor: Yes, and that's part of why we're being thorough with you. Family history matters. Have you connected with him about all this?

Patient: I have. He's been worried but supportive. Honestly, my mom's been more anxious than him.

Doctor: Understandable. Let me listen quickly. (pause) Heart regular, slower than before because of the metoprolol, which is what we want. Lungs clear. No swelling.

Patient: Good.

Doctor: Any new questions or concerns I can help with today?

Patient: I'm not sure if I should keep walking. The cardiologist said light activity is okay. I don't want to overdo it.

Doctor: Light walking is excellent. Avoid anything that brings on the chest discomfort. If you have to stop because of symptoms, you've gone too far. Use the nitroglycerin if needed, and call us if you have to use it more than once or twice in a week, or if it's not working as well.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: After the CT angiogram, Dr. Patel will want to see you to go over the results. Once she has a plan, she'll loop me in, and we'll regroup here within a few weeks.

Patient: Sounds good.

Doctor: Hang in there, Sarah. You're doing the right things.

Patient: Thanks, doctor.

visits.chief_complaint: Follow-up after coronary catheterization and stent placement.
visits.transcript:
Doctor: Sarah, welcome back. How are you?

Patient: Pretty good, considering. The stent is in. I'm still getting used to everything.

Doctor: I have Dr. Patel's report. You had the catheterization on November 12th. They found a 75% lesion in the mid-LAD and placed a drug-eluting stent. The right coronary had some mild disease, and the circumflex was fine. No procedural complications.

Patient: Yeah. Honestly, the procedure itself wasn't as bad as I'd feared. They went in through my wrist, I was awake but sedated. I was home that evening.

Doctor: Modern interventional cardiology is remarkable. How have you been since?

Patient: The chest tightness is gone. Completely. I can't believe how much better I feel.

Doctor: That's wonderful. And the shortness of breath?

Patient: Much better. I can walk again without thinking about it.

Doctor: BP today is 122 over 74. Pulse 62. Both look great. You're now on aspirin and clopidogrel for the stent — the dual antiplatelet therapy. Are you tolerating those?

Patient: I bruise more easily, I've noticed. Otherwise fine.

Doctor: That's expected. You'll be on the clopidogrel for at least a year, and the aspirin indefinitely. We never stop those without coordinating with cardiology. Even for procedures or surgeries.

Patient: Right, they emphasized that.

Doctor: Are you still on the metoprolol, the lisinopril, the atorvastatin?

Patient: Yes. They actually upped the atorvastatin to 40 milligrams.

Doctor: Good. We want your LDL well under 70 now. We'll recheck the lipid panel at three months.

Patient: Okay.

Doctor: Any new symptoms — chest discomfort, palpitations, leg swelling, bleeding?

Patient: No swelling. No bleeding other than bruising. Heart feels normal.

Doctor: Cardiac rehab — did Dr. Patel refer you?

Patient: Yes, I start next week. Twelve weeks of supervised exercise.

Doctor: Excellent. That program will give you a lot back, both physically and mentally. People who go through it have better long-term outcomes.

Patient: I'm looking forward to it actually. I've been so cautious about activity.

Doctor: It'll be a structured way to rebuild confidence. Now, lifestyle — diet, weight, alcohol?

Patient: I've been eating better since all this started. More vegetables, less takeout. I've lost about four pounds. Cutting back on the wine.

Doctor: All wonderful changes. Keep them going.

Patient: I will.

Doctor: Let me listen. (pause) Heart regular, no murmurs. Lungs clear. Wrist looks well-healed. Any continued tenderness there?

Patient: Just a little when I press on it.

Doctor: That'll fade. Any concerns about mood — depression, anxiety after all this? It's common.

Patient: I had some anxiety the first couple weeks. It's settled. Honestly, I feel relieved more than anything. Like we caught it.

Doctor: We did. Many people don't get this kind of warning, or don't act on it. You did both.

Patient: My family doctor for that.

Doctor: We did it together. Let's see you again in eight weeks for the lipid recheck and a general follow-up. Call sooner if anything new comes up — chest discomfort, shortness of breath, or any bleeding concerns.

Patient: Will do. Thank you.

Doctor: Hi Sarah. It's been about ten weeks since I saw you. How are you doing?

Patient: Really well, actually. I finished cardiac rehab two weeks ago.

Doctor: Excellent. How was it?

Patient: Hard at first, but I genuinely enjoyed it by the end. They had us doing treadmill, the stationary bike, some resistance work. I can do things now I couldn't do six months ago.

Doctor: That's wonderful. Any chest discomfort, shortness of breath, palpitations, anything since I saw you?

Patient: Nothing. Honestly, it's like the whole thing is starting to feel like it happened to someone else.

Doctor: BP today is 118 over 72. Pulse 64. Beautiful numbers. Let me run through your medications — aspirin 81, clopidogrel 75, metoprolol 25 twice a day, lisinopril 30, atorvastatin 40, omeprazole as needed?

Patient: That's right. I take them all in the morning except the metoprolol, which I split, and the atorvastatin at night.

Doctor: Good system. Lipid panel from last week — your LDL is now 62, down from 156 a year ago. Total cholesterol 138. HDL 52. Triglycerides 110. These are excellent.

Patient: Wow.

Doctor: That's the statin doing its job, plus your dietary changes. A1c is 5.4, back in the normal range — even better than last year.

Patient: I've been more careful with sweets and snacks too.

Doctor: It shows. How's your weight?

Patient: I've lost about nine pounds total since all this started.

Doctor: Excellent. How about the mood, the anxiety?

Patient: Much better. Cardiac rehab was helpful for that too. Talking to other people who'd been through similar things.

Doctor: That community piece is underrated.

Patient: Yeah. There was one woman — she's about my age, had a much bigger event than I did. She's doing great now. It puts things in perspective.

Doctor: It does. Family — how are they?

Patient: My husband has been amazing. My daughter came home for break and we walked together every day she was here.

Doctor: That's lovely. Have you spoken to your father about what you went through?

Patient: We talk every couple of weeks. He's relieved. He keeps reminding me to take my pills, like he's the doctor now.

Doctor: That's sweet. Let me listen quickly. (pause) Heart sounds regular, no murmurs or extra sounds. Lungs clear. No swelling. Wrist scar looks good.

Patient: Good.

Doctor: Looking back at the whole picture — the symptoms started so vaguely, and we worked our way through it. The stress echo, the CT angiogram, the catheterization in November, the stent in the LAD — those findings really validated the slow burn we'd been chasing.

Patient: I think about that sometimes. If I hadn't mentioned the chest tightness back at the second visit.

Doctor: You did, and we kept circling back to it. That matters. Now, going forward — we'll see you every three months for the next year, alternating with cardiology. Annual exercise stress test, lipid panel every six months. Stay on the dual antiplatelet through November at minimum. And keep doing what you're doing — exercise, diet, the medications.

Patient: I will.

Doctor: Any questions for me today?

Patient: Just one. Can I travel? My husband and I want to take a trip in the spring.

Doctor: Absolutely. Bring your medication list, know where the nearest hospital is at your destination, but you can travel. On long flights, get up and walk every couple of hours, drink water.

Patient: Perfect.

Doctor: Take care, Sarah. You're doing great.

Patient: Thanks, doctor. See you in three months.
